title: Clone Graph
code: var map = new Dictionary<Node, Node>(); // orig->clone

frames.caption: Goal: deep-copy this undirected graph. We DFS from node 1, keeping a map original -> clone.
frames.note: A deep copy must reproduce every node and every edge with all-new objects. Cloning naively recurses forever on cycles (this graph is one big cycle). The fix is one hash map from each original node to its clone, which also serves as the visited set. We build it with DFS from the entry node.

frames.caption: Dfs(1): node 1 is new → create clone, store map[1] = clone, THEN recurse.
frames.note: The node is not in the map yet, so make its clone now and record original -> clone in the map BEFORE touching any neighbor. Recording first is the crucial ordering: when a neighbor later recurses back to this node (a cycle will), the map lookup above finds the clone instead of cloning again and looping. Create, map, THEN recurse.

frames.caption: Dfs(2): node 2 is new → create clone, store map[2] = clone, THEN recurse.
frames.note: The node is not in the map yet, so make its clone now and record original -> clone in the map BEFORE touching any neighbor. Recording first is the crucial ordering: when a neighbor later recurses back to this node (a cycle will), the map lookup above finds the clone instead of cloning again and looping. Create, map, THEN recurse.

frames.caption: Dfs(1): node 1 is already in the map → return its existing clone, no re-clone.
frames.note: Before cloning anything, look it up in the map. The map original -> clone is ALSO the visited set: if this node is already a key, its clone exists and is (or will be) fully wired, so we return that same clone instead of building a second copy. This single check is what stops a cycle from recursing forever AND what makes every edge point at one shared clone per original.

frames.caption: Wire clone 2 → clone 1: Dfs(1) gave back clone 1, add it to clone 2's neighbors.
frames.note: Recurse into a neighbor: that call returns the neighbor's clone (newly built, or fetched from the map if already done), and we add it to THIS clone's neighbor list. Edges are rebuilt in clone-space pointing only at clones — never at originals. Each original edge is handled exactly once per direction, so the whole clone is O(V + E).

frames.caption: Dfs(3): node 3 is new → create clone, store map[3] = clone, THEN recurse.
frames.note: The node is not in the map yet, so make its clone now and record original -> clone in the map BEFORE touching any neighbor. Recording first is the crucial ordering: when a neighbor later recurses back to this node (a cycle will), the map lookup above finds the clone instead of cloning again and looping. Create, map, THEN recurse.

frames.caption: Dfs(2): node 2 is already in the map → return its existing clone, no re-clone.
frames.note: Before cloning anything, look it up in the map. The map original -> clone is ALSO the visited set: if this node is already a key, its clone exists and is (or will be) fully wired, so we return that same clone instead of building a second copy. This single check is what stops a cycle from recursing forever AND what makes every edge point at one shared clone per original.

frames.caption: Wire clone 3 → clone 2: Dfs(2) gave back clone 2, add it to clone 3's neighbors.
frames.note: Recurse into a neighbor: that call returns the neighbor's clone (newly built, or fetched from the map if already done), and we add it to THIS clone's neighbor list. Edges are rebuilt in clone-space pointing only at clones — never at originals. Each original edge is handled exactly once per direction, so the whole clone is O(V + E).

frames.caption: Dfs(4): node 4 is new → create clone, store map[4] = clone, THEN recurse.
frames.note: The node is not in the map yet, so make its clone now and record original -> clone in the map BEFORE touching any neighbor. Recording first is the crucial ordering: when a neighbor later recurses back to this node (a cycle will), the map lookup above finds the clone instead of cloning again and looping. Create, map, THEN recurse.

frames.caption: Dfs(1): node 1 is already in the map → return its existing clone, no re-clone.
frames.note: Before cloning anything, look it up in the map. The map original -> clone is ALSO the visited set: if this node is already a key, its clone exists and is (or will be) fully wired, so we return that same clone instead of building a second copy. This single check is what stops a cycle from recursing forever AND what makes every edge point at one shared clone per original.

frames.caption: Wire clone 4 → clone 1: Dfs(1) gave back clone 1, add it to clone 4's neighbors.
frames.note: Recurse into a neighbor: that call returns the neighbor's clone (newly built, or fetched from the map if already done), and we add it to THIS clone's neighbor list. Edges are rebuilt in clone-space pointing only at clones — never at originals. Each original edge is handled exactly once per direction, so the whole clone is O(V + E).

frames.caption: Dfs(3): node 3 is already in the map → return its existing clone, no re-clone.
frames.note: Before cloning anything, look it up in the map. The map original -> clone is ALSO the visited set: if this node is already a key, its clone exists and is (or will be) fully wired, so we return that same clone instead of building a second copy. This single check is what stops a cycle from recursing forever AND what makes every edge point at one shared clone per original.

frames.caption: Wire clone 4 → clone 3: Dfs(3) gave back clone 3, add it to clone 4's neighbors.
frames.note: Recurse into a neighbor: that call returns the neighbor's clone (newly built, or fetched from the map if already done), and we add it to THIS clone's neighbor list. Edges are rebuilt in clone-space pointing only at clones — never at originals. Each original edge is handled exactly once per direction, so the whole clone is O(V + E).

frames.caption: Dfs(4) is complete: all of node 4's neighbors are cloned and wired. Return clone 4.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this node has been cloned and wired, so its clone is complete; return it up the recursion. Because we mapped the clone before recursing, any later visitor short-circuits at the map. When the first call (the entry node) returns, the full deep copy is done: same shape, all-new nodes, O(V) space for the map plus the recursion stack.

frames.caption: Wire clone 3 → clone 4: Dfs(4) gave back clone 4, add it to clone 3's neighbors.
frames.note: Recurse into a neighbor: that call returns the neighbor's clone (newly built, or fetched from the map if already done), and we add it to THIS clone's neighbor list. Edges are rebuilt in clone-space pointing only at clones — never at originals. Each original edge is handled exactly once per direction, so the whole clone is O(V + E).

frames.caption: Wire clone 1 → clone 2: Dfs(2) gave back clone 2, add it to clone 1's neighbors.
frames.note: Recurse into a neighbor: that call returns the neighbor's clone (newly built, or fetched from the map if already done), and we add it to THIS clone's neighbor list. frames.caption: Wire clone 1 → clone 4: Dfs(4) gave back clone 4, add it to clone 1's neighbors.
frames.note: Recurse into a neighbor: that call returns the neighbor's clone (newly built, or fetched from the map if already done), and we add it to THIS clone's neighbor list. Edges are rebuilt in clone-space pointing only at clones — never at originals. Each original edge is handled exactly once per direction, so the whole clone is O(V + E).

frames.caption: Dfs(1) is complete: all of node 1's neighbors are cloned and wired. Return clone 1.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this node has been cloned and wired, so its clone is complete; return it up the recursion. Because we mapped the clone before recursing, any later visitor short-circuits at the map. When the first call (the entry node) returns, the full deep copy is done: same shape, all-new nodes, O(V) space for the map plus the recursion stack.

frames.caption: Done: every node and edge is deep-copied. The map holds all 4 original → clone pairs; the clone graph mirrors the original with all-new nodes.
frames.note: The top-level Dfs(node) has returned the clone of the entry node, and through it the whole new graph. Total work is O(V + E): each node is created once (map check blocks repeats) and each edge wired once per direction. Space is O(V) for the map plus the DFS recursion stack.
